title: Annex — The starting brief
meta.desc: The original Tower Duel brief, presented as a torn, annotated ticket: comments, critiques and good points. How to describe a project well before coding.
hero.title: The starting brief
hero.lead: Before the first line of code, there's a sentence. Here is the original brief of Tower Duel, torn and annotated — and what it teaches us about describing a project well.
card.title: The starting brief
card.desc: The spec that started it all — presented, annotated and critiqued: how to describe a project well.
s1.lead: A project doesn't start with code, but with an intention. Here is the starting ticket, piece by piece, with the comments slipped between the scraps.
s1.p1.l1: New project, versioned on GitHub. First attempt with Phaser JS (latest version): a classic of the old artillery games.
s1.p1.l2: Two players. One tower on each side of a landscape, one cannon per tower. Each player sets the power and the angle of their shot. The wind (force + direction) changes on every volley. Score shown at the top.
s1.p1.l3: Before the match: number of rounds and player names. The landscape changes on every round.
s1.p1.l4: The twist: both players set up their shot at the same time, then the shots fire together.
s1.note1.pa: “An old classic” is enough to place the genre (artillery); and “the twist…” immediately says what makes THIS game special: simultaneous aiming. Reference + intention = a clear heading.
s1.note1.pb: “latest version of Phaser”: at the time of the project, Phaser 4 had just come out (little documentation, shifting APIs). Better to pin a precise version.
s1.p2.lot2: batch 2 — biomes chosen before the match + modernised graphics & sound effects (particles, parallax…).
s1.p2.lot3: batch 3 — a TV that connects becomes a spectator (no view of the setups, no command interface); up to 2 phones/tablets take over a player's controls; once everyone has confirmed, the shots fire.
s1.p2.lot4: batch 4 — Worms-style, the landscape takes damage (the “obstacle terrain” as well as the “scenery”).
s1.note2.p: From the minimal playable (batch 1) to the rich (batch 4): we have something running very early, then we stack on top. This is exactly the right method.
s1.p3.intro: Three roles, set from the start. The multi-screen part only arrives at batch 3, but the brief defines them right away:
s1.p3.role1: Host & spectator: displays the match, no shot controls.
s1.p3.role2: A player's controller: sets the angle and power, confirms the shot.
s1.p3.role3: Either: host (like a TV) or join as a player.
s1.note3.h4: ✓ The roles written from the brief
s1.note3.p: Defining the devices at the start — even if nothing uses them before batch 3 — avoids rediscovering the constraints (“the TV must command nothing”) at coding time.
s1.p4.intro: Strict vocabulary: a single word per concept, no synonyms allowed. In case of ambiguity, whoever builds it checks back with the author of the brief; any new word is confirmed before entering the dictionary.
s1.p4.dd1: a player's structure; it carries the cannon.
s1.p4.dd2: the weapon mounted on the tower; fires a projectile.
s1.p4.dd3: one exchange of simultaneous shots. The wind changes on every volley.
s1.p4.dd4: a winnable game; their number is fixed before playing.
s1.p4.dd5: a horizontal gust (force + direction), redrawn on every volley.
s1.p4.dd6: the theme of the landscape (meadow, desert, tundra, volcano…).
s1.note4.p: Without one, “tower” means both the structure and the turn of play; “round” and “volley” get mixed up. One word = one sense: the code, the screens and the discussions finally speak the same language.
s1.p5.l1: Code & documentation in English (impersonal style, infinitive). Discussions happen in French.
s1.p5.l2: One commit per batch; clear scope; each batch is validated before the next.
s1.note5.pa: Language of the code, commit rhythm, scope, per-batch validation: everyone knows how to work together.
s1.note5.pb: The win criteria and balancing (health points, ammo…) only arrived later. Ideally, to be fixed right from the brief.
s2.lead: A good brief holds in four ingredients:
s2.callout.nerd: For the curious. A brief says the WHAT and the WHY, not the HOW. The “how” (files, algorithms) is the job of whoever builds it.
s2.callout.try: Your turn! Write the brief of a mini-game in 4 lines: a reference, your twist, your dictionary (3 words), and 3 batches from the simplest to the richest.
